stanceNote: A comparative hermeneutic atlas. Six vocabularies side-by-side - not an empirical map of a universal psyche. Convergence is partly shared intellectual descent, not independent corroboration.

confidenceTiers.canonical: Author-cited within the system's published primary literature.
confidenceTiers.strong: Credentialed secondary scholarship (Nichols, Pollack, Vogler-on-Campbell, etc.).
confidenceTiers.moderate: Defensible by structural similarity + hermeneutic convergence, but not author-endorsed.
confidenceTiers.contested: Mapping is actively disputed in the scholarship.

clusters.theme: The Sovereign - Order, Authority, Centering
clusters.description: The centering energy that orders the field, blesses, and holds the boundary. Not domination but the still point that makes a kingdom possible.
clusters.editorialNote: Mission 8's tightest triangle (King/Ruler/Emperor). Challenger and Reformer removed: Type 8 is autonomy-defense; Type 1's authority is rule-derived.
clusters.adversarialNote: Family-resemblance, not convergence. Four different theories of what authority IS.

archetypes.note: Fullness - the blessing centered self
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Moore & Gillette, King Warrior Magician Lover (1990), ch. 3–4
archetypes.dissent: Moore restricted KWML to mature masculine; gender-neutral use erases stated scope.
archetypes.editorialNote: The King is centered blessing, not dominating power.

archetypes.note: Self cluster - claim dominion, take responsibility
archetypes.adversarialNote: Pre-capture Pearson (1986) did not include Ruler; this is 1991-expansion material.

archetypes.note: IV - structure, law, the enthroned boundary
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)

archetypes.note: Te-dom commander - systems-level authority
archetypes.scholarlyCitation: Jung, Psychological Types (1921); Myers, Gifts Differing (1980)
archetypes.dissent: MBTI psychometric status contested.

archetypes.note: Te/Si executive - enforces the working order
The Sun at the center — not domination but radiance, the self that warms a kingdom by being lit.
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.
archetypes.adversarialNote: The Golden Dawn assigns Leo not to the throne but to Strength (VIII) — the tamed lion, mastery of the animal, not command of a court.

archetypes.note: Saturn's elder — Greene's Senex, the one born old who governs by gravity and time.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Greene, Saturn: A New Look at an Old Devil (1976)
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.
archetypes.adversarialNote: This is the map's sharpest internal argument: Greene reads Capricorn as the wise Senex-Sovereign, but the Golden Dawn gives Capricorn the Devil (XV) — the same ambition read as bondage to matter.

alternativeMapping.argument: By the Golden Dawn attribution Capricorn is the Devil — placing the sign among the Antagonists, the exact inverse of Greene's Senex-Sovereign reading.

clusters.theme: The Warrior - Purposeful Action, Discipline, Courage
clusters.description: Directed force in service of a cause larger than self. The disciplined edge that cuts what must be cut and protects what must be protected.
clusters.editorialNote: Jungian/Pearson Hero moved to Boy-Hero primary - pre-initiation striving, not post-initiation service.
clusters.adversarialNote: KWML Warrior is MAN-level; Pearson's Hero is EGO-level. Different sides of the initiation line.

archetypes.note: Fullness - disciplined, transpersonally-committed action
The central protagonist who crosses the threshold
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Campbell, Hero with a Thousand Faces (1949)
archetypes.scholarlyCitation: Vogler, The Writer's Journey (2007) - Hero as flexible function, not fixed identity
archetypes.dissent: Murdock rejects the masculine-coded arc; Campbell's Jungianism contested (Segal, Rensma, IAJS).

archetypes.note: VII - directed momentum, will harnessing opposing forces
Type 8 - confronts directly, breaks down resistance
archetypes.dissent: Type 8's core is autonomy-defense (Horney against), not transpersonal commitment. Better fit: Rebel.

archetypes.note: Se-dom operator - decisive action under fire
Mars's child — the will to begin, the cut that sets the world moving.
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.
archetypes.adversarialNote: The Golden Dawn placed Aries not in war but in sovereignty — its Emperor (IV) — reading cardinal fire as the ordering father, not the soldier.

alternativeMapping.argument: By the Golden Dawn attribution Aries is the Emperor, routing it to the Sovereign rather than the Warrior.

clusters.theme: The Sage-Magician - Truth-Seeking and Transformation
clusters.description: The knower-transformer. Sage seeks clarity for its own sake; Magician bends pattern to bring change. Same territory, two stances.
clusters.editorialNote: MERGED. Sage and Magician were near-duplicates in feature space with mutually primary members. Presented as two lenses on shared territory.
clusters.adversarialNote: Tension is between truth-as-freedom (Sage) and truth-as-leverage (Magician). Named, not collapsed.

archetypes.note: Fullness - transformative ritual elder and seer
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Moore & Gillette (1990), ch. 6; Moore, The Magician Within (1993)

archetypes.note: Self cluster - truth will set you free
Self cluster - make dreams real through inner power
I - directed will, the four tools in hand
archetypes.scholarlyCitation: Nichols (1980); Pollack, Seventy-Eight Degrees of Wisdom (1980)

archetypes.note: II - inner knowing, intuitive gnosis (Sage lens)
archetypes.dissent: Pollack, a trans author, later complicated her own gendered Jungian framing.

archetypes.note: IX - wisdom from solitude (both lenses)
Type 5 - mastery through knowing, the hidden observer
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Riso & Hudson, Wisdom of the Enneagram (1999)

archetypes.note: Ni-dom - long-sight pattern-holder
Ti-dom - theorist of underlying structure
The Hermit's lamp (IX) and Mercury's craft, converging — analysis as a spiritual discipline.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: Precession has pulled the tropical signs ~24° off the constellations, so sidereal (Vedic) practice already disputes what sign this is.
archetypes.editorialNote: A rare clean convergence: the Golden Dawn (Hermit) and the archetypal reading (Mercury-earth craft) point to the same cluster.

archetypes.note: Pluto's alchemist — the one who goes down into the fixed water and comes back having transmuted it.
archetypes.dissent: Astrology's sign-claims fail controlled testing (Carlson, Nature 1985); read here as projected psychology, not celestial fact.

clusters.theme: The Lover - Connection, Passion, Embodied Feeling
clusters.description: The energy of communion - sensual, empathic, feelingful. Four different theories of what love IS, held together as family-resemblance.
clusters.editorialNote: Each entry names its specific theory: Pearson's Soul-cluster intimacy; Tarot's conscious CHOICE; Type 4's shame-identity; MBTI's sensory-embodiment.
clusters.adversarialNote: Lover anchors the fourth affect-center (desire/eros) that the Enneagram's three leave homeless.

archetypes.note: Fullness - embodied empathy, the saint of sensuality
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Moore & Gillette (1990), ch. 7; Moore, The Lover Within (1995)

archetypes.note: Soul cluster - intimacy, devotion, aesthetic bliss
VI - conscious CHOICE of value; alignment, not infatuation
archetypes.editorialNote: Common 'union' reading leans on name, not function.

archetypes.note: Type 4 - shame-based identity-longing; depth-of-feeling as self-definition
archetypes.dissent: Type 4's core is shame, not eros.

archetypes.note: Type 2 - relational warmth, gives to be loved
Fi/Ne - enthusiastic romantic
Se/Fi - sensual present-moment connector
Fi-dom - the poet of felt experience
Venus at the scales — the self completed only in relation, the beauty that is also a judgment.
archetypes.dissent: Astrology's sign-claims fail controlled testing (Carlson, Nature 1985); read here as projected psychology, not celestial fact.

clusters.theme: The Innocent - Pre-Initiation and Earned
clusters.description: Every individuation model names TWO innocences: the pre-initiation openness (Fool, Divine Child) and the post-initiation radiance (Sun, Jester-at-rest). Not the same.
clusters.editorialNote: Added Sun (XIX) and ISFJ per Mission 8. Two-part structure now explicit.
clusters.adversarialNote: Previous version overweighted pre-initiation and ignored the return to innocence.

archetypes.note: Boy in fullness (King family) - radiant wholeness before corruption
Ego cluster - faith, simplicity, the trusting beginner
0 - PRE-initiation unconditioned potential
archetypes.scholarlyCitation: Nichols (1980); Pollack (1980) on the Fool's Journey

archetypes.note: XIX - EARNED innocence; post-individuation radiant wholeness
Type 9 - merging harmony, the unfallen garden
Si/Fe - trust + belonging; Mission 8 feature-vector match
Fi-dom idealist - keeper of untouched values
Fi/Se - quiet purity, guileless feeling
Neptune's dreamer — the one who believes past all evidence, the wound and the grace of it.
archetypes.dissent: Astrology's sign-claims fail controlled testing (Carlson, Nature 1985); read here as projected psychology, not celestial fact.

clusters.theme: The Explorer - Freedom, Autonomy, Self-Discovery
clusters.description: The restless drive toward horizon. Refuses the small room, tests the world, builds selfhood through venturing.
clusters.editorialNote: Enthusiast demoted to secondary: Pearson's Explorer is individuation-quest; Type 7 is fear-avoidance escape. Not the same.
clusters.adversarialNote: Explorer and Enthusiast code similarly in feature space but come from opposite origins.

archetypes.note: Soul cluster - find the way, refuse captivity
The call answered - leaving the ordinary world
0 - the leap into the unknown
Type 7 - appetite as ESCAPE from limitation
archetypes.dissent: Riso: Type 7's core is fear-of-limitation, not freedom-toward-self.

archetypes.note: Type 5 - intellectual exploration
The call itself - echo in this cluster
Ne-dom - chases every unopened door
Ne/Fi - possibility-seeker with heart
Ne/Ti - speculative cartographer
Jupiter's arrow — the Seeker who aims past the horizon and mistakes the aiming for arrival.
archetypes.dissent: Astrology's sign-claims fail controlled testing (Carlson, Nature 1985); read here as projected psychology, not celestial fact.

clusters.theme: The Rebel - Disruption, Revolution, Outrageous Honesty
clusters.description: The one who breaks the corrupted pattern. AGENTS of breaking (Rebel, Challenger, Trickster) and EVENTS of breaking (Tower) co-present here.
clusters.editorialNote: Agent-vs-event distinction named. Tower is event-kin; Rebel is agent.
clusters.adversarialNote: Four incompatible theories of what 'breaking' means. Held as family-resemblance.

archetypes.note: Soul cluster (Outlaw/Destroyer) - revolutionary energy
Type 8 - confronts authority, autonomy-defense as protective-rebellion
XVI - EVENT-archetype: liberating collapse
Disrupts the old order - dramatic FUNCTION, not fixed identity
XV - the chains that must be seen to be broken
Ne/Ti - the provocateur
Se/Ti - the rule-breaker at the edge
Uranus's lightning — Tarnas's Promethean sign, the one who steals fire and breaks the given order.
archetypes.dissent: Even Tarnas, sign-astrology's most rigorous modern defender, found no signal in the signs and staked his archetypal case on the planets alone.

clusters.theme: The Caregiver - Nurturance, Protection, Generosity
clusters.description: The one who holds, feeds, protects. Service as love made visible. The most gender-coded cluster in the corpus - Bolen's Goddesses stand as counter-canon.
clusters.editorialNote: Gender-coding named inline. Counter-canon (Bolen 1984, Estés 1992) linked as first-class parallel. Ally demoted to weakest tertiary per Vogler's function-not-energy distinction.
clusters.adversarialNote: Refusing to name the gendering would repeat the erasure Murdock, Bolen, and Estés identified.

archetypes.note: Ego cluster - care for others, nurture against harm
archetypes.dissent: Bolen (1984): Caregiver elides Demeter (loss-in-motherhood), Hera (partnership), Hestia (hearth-as-selfhood).

archetypes.note: Type 2 - gives to be loved
III - generative embodiment (also primary in Creator)
Fe-dom provider
Si/Fe guardian
Fe/Ni - harmonizer
Loyal support - but Ally is relational FUNCTION, not nurturant energy
archetypes.dissent: Vogler: role a character wears, not type of energy. Weak fit.

archetypes.note: The Moon's tide — the Mother who makes a shell for the soft thing and calls it home.
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.
archetypes.adversarialNote: The Golden Dawn gives Cancer the Chariot (VII) — a warrior sign, all armor — reading the crab's shell as a war-cart, not a cradle.

alternativeMapping.argument: Golden Dawn attribution (Cancer=Chariot) reads the sign as armored will, placing it nearer the Warrior.

clusters.theme: The Jester - Play, Lightness, Sacred Irreverence
clusters.description: The holy fool. Punctures pretension with humor, reveals truth sideways, teaches that joy is also serious. Three theories of play held here: Jester (integration), Trickster (function), Fool (pre-initiation).
clusters.editorialNote: Enthusiast demoted to secondary (double-primary with Explorer was too much).

archetypes.note: Self cluster - played-through-the-fire lightness
Sacred fool; boundary-crossing dramatic function
0 - PRE-initiation unconditioned play
archetypes.editorialNote: Fool and Jester are two ends of the innocence arc meeting as play.

archetypes.note: Type 7 - reframing-as-joy; fear-avoidance as lightness
archetypes.dissent: Riso: Type 7's joy is partly defense.

archetypes.note: Se/Fi - the stage-born entertainer
Ne/Ti - the witty provocateur
Ne/Fi - the irrepressible enthusiast
Mercury's tongue — the trickster of the crossroads, quick, doubled, never only one thing.
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.
archetypes.adversarialNote: The Golden Dawn routes Gemini to the Lovers (VI) — the choice at the crossroads — reading the twins as union, not mischief.

alternativeMapping.argument: Golden Dawn attribution (Gemini=Lovers) places the sign in the Lover cluster.

clusters.theme: The Creator - Imagination, Vision, Enduring Form
clusters.description: The maker who brings something into being. Vision crystallized into form.
clusters.editorialNote: EMPRESS added as primary - Mission 10 'clearest mapping error' corrected. Her coreTheme is generative embodiment: maker, not only mother.

archetypes.note: Soul cluster - if you can imagine it, you can create it
III - GENERATIVE embodiment; maker as much as mother
Type 4 - unique expression, identity through making
I - will shaping matter
XVII - creative faith, hope poured into form
Ni-dom visionary
Fi/Ne - interior world made into art
Ne/Fi - generative enthusiast
The Star (XVII) — the Golden Dawn's Aquarius, hope poured out, the vision after the Tower falls.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.

clusters.theme: The Everyman - Belonging, Solidarity, Ordinariness
clusters.description: The energy of the regular person. Refuses elevation, prizes fit with the group, honors the ordinary decencies.
clusters.editorialNote: MBTI Sentinels (SJ) carry Keirsey's concrete-tradition theory under their own name; Pearson's Orphan/Everyman is Ego-cluster belonging. Partial overlap acknowledged.

archetypes.note: Ego cluster - all people are created equal
Type 6 - bonds of trust, fidelity to the group
Type 9 - merges with the group
Si/Fe - quiet backbone of community
archetypes.dissent: Sentinel Si-dom tradition-keeper is its own theory.

archetypes.note: Fe/Si - the host who remembers everyone's name
Si-dom - institutional-memory function
archetypes.dissent: ISTJ is tradition-keeper; mapping to Everyman loses institutional-memory.

archetypes.note: The body's contentment — Venusian earth that asks only to have, to hold, to remain.
archetypes.dissent: Astrology's sign-claims fail controlled testing (Carlson, Nature 1985); read here as projected psychology, not celestial fact.

clusters.theme: The Antagonists - Archetypes Whose Primary Function is Opposition
clusters.description: Narrow replacement for the old Shadow cluster: only figures whose core function is to oppose, obstruct, or bind. Shadow-as-TYPOLOGY (bipolar poles, unipolar distortion, reversal, stack-inversion) lives on /about/shadow-structures.
clusters.editorialNote: Old Shadow cluster was a category error - mixing KWML's bipolar shadow STRUCTURE with Hero's-Journey Shadow as character-role with Tarot Devil as event-symbol with Enneagram disintegration as transit-state. DISSOLVED.
clusters.adversarialNote: Most severe Mission 10 critique; conceded in full.

archetypes.note: The antagonist mask - the villain who mirrors the hero
XV - bound shadow; event-symbol of bondage
XVIII - dreaming unconscious; obstacle of uncertainty
The Devil's chain (XV) — ambition hardened into bondage, the mountain climbed for its own cold sake.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.
archetypes.editorialNote: The same sign sits in Sovereign (Greene's Senex) and here in Antagonists (the Golden Dawn's Devil) — kept in both on purpose. Which one owns Capricorn is a live question, not an error.

alternativeMapping.argument: Greene's Saturn reads Capricorn as the Senex-Sovereign, not the Devil — the opposite cluster.

clusters.theme: The Shapeshifter - Ambiguity, Transformation, Anima/Animus
clusters.description: The figure whose allegiance and form keep shifting. Jung/Campbell's anima/animus theory is critiqued as heterosexist (Hopcke 1989); framing retained with caveat surfaced.
clusters.editorialNote: Added Lover (anima-paradigm in KWML and Pearson), added Wheel of Fortune. Demoted Achiever - Type 3 is image-adaptation, not liminal ambiguity.

archetypes.note: Anima/animus projection - ally or adversary, unclear
archetypes.dissent: Hopcke, Jung Jungians and Homosexuality (1989): anima/animus framing is heterosexist.

archetypes.note: XVIII - illusion, shifting form, dreamlike uncertainty
X - fortune as shape-shifter of circumstance
Anima-paradigm in Jungian-Pearson; Hopcke caveat applies
Ne/Fi - the fluid, context-adapting spirit
Ne/Ti - devil's-advocate shapeshifter of argument
The twins as a diagnosis, not a decoration — the self that is always at least two.
archetypes.dissent: Astrology's sign-claims fail controlled testing (Carlson, Nature 1985); read here as projected psychology, not celestial fact.

archetypes.note: The Moon's water (XVIII) — dissolution of edges, the self that leaks into everything it touches.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: Precession has pulled the tropical signs ~24° off the constellations, so sidereal (Vedic) practice already disputes what sign this is.

clusters.theme: The Threshold Guardian - Tests, Gatekeeping, Rule-Enforcement
clusters.description: The one who bars the gate - not to defeat the seeker but to test readiness. Rule-as-authority rather than authority-from-center.
clusters.editorialNote: Added Justice (XI) and Reformer (Type 1). Both were routed elsewhere without noticing that rule-enforcement is threshold-function.

archetypes.note: Blocks the uncommitted, initiates the ready
Type 1 - moral authority DERIVED from compliance with a rule; gatekeeper-of-standards
XI - weighed boundary, the edge that cuts clean
VIII - taming the inner beast
Boy-Warrior - must be overcome for maturity
Type 6 - the wary gatekeeper
Si/Te - rule-keeper at the gate
Te/Si - enforcer of standards
Justice (XI) — the Golden Dawn's Libra, the scales that weigh what may pass and what may not.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.

clusters.theme: The Herald - The Call, Disruption of Homeostasis
clusters.description: The messenger whose arrival shatters the ordinary. The call can be refused but never unheard.
clusters.editorialNote: Added Tower (most violent herald in the deck). Campbell-derived frame; see /about/heros-journey for contested-Jungianism caveat.

archetypes.note: Initiates the call, announces the change
archetypes.dissent: Campbell's Jungian credentials contested (Segal, Rensma, IAJS).

archetypes.note: XX - the inner calling (dual-home with Death-Rebirth)
XVI - violent herald; the call that cannot be refused
X - change arrives, the wheel turns
Type 7 - answers every call immediately
Fe/Ni - summons others
Ne/Fi - enthusiastic awakener

clusters.theme: Death & Rebirth - Necessary Ending, Transformation
clusters.description: The passage that requires loss. KWML's death-rebirth is initiatory-developmental (boy→man); Tarot's Death is broadly transformational. Different theories, co-presented.
clusters.editorialNote: Added Judgement (XX - coreTheme literally 'vocation, reckoning, rebirth'). Description names the KWML-developmental vs Tarot-general distinction.

archetypes.note: INITIATION: the boy must die for the man to emerge
INITIATION: striving ego surrenders to the Warrior
INITIATION: clever boy yields to the wise Magician
INITIATION: hungry boy becomes the generous Lover
XIII - GENERAL transformation: necessary ending
XX - vocation, reckoning, rebirth
XVI - sudden structural collapse, forced rebirth
XII - willing surrender, world inverted
Integration arrow (to 1) - artist yields to discipline
archetypes.dissent: Naranjo later disavowed the arrows. Associative edge.

archetypes.note: Ni-attunement to cycles - ASSOCIATIVE, not structural
Ni/Te - strategist of necessary demolition - associative
Death (XIII) — the Golden Dawn's Scorpio, the fixed water that will not survive unchanged.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: Astrology's sign-claims fail controlled testing (Carlson, Nature 1985); read here as projected psychology, not celestial fact.
archetypes.editorialNote: The bridge and the archetypal reading agree here: Scorpio is transformation by descent, whichever door you enter through.

clusters.theme: Integration & Wholeness - The Completed Self
clusters.description: Opposites held together. Four systems, four different end-states (Pearson wholeness; Campbell return-with-boon; Riso Level 9; Tarot World). Axis continuous, destinations not identical.
clusters.editorialNote: Added Temperance (LITERALLY named for alchemical integration), Emperor (integrated sovereign), Hierophant (integrated tradition-bearer).

archetypes.note: Quaternio in balance - King as integrating center
Warrior energy under sovereign service
Magician energy under sovereign service
Lover energy under sovereign service
XXI - completion, the dance of the four in unity
XIV - alchemical integration; LITERALLY named for this function
XIX - earned-innocence as integration (dual-home with Innocent)
IV - integrated sovereign-at-the-boundary (third-phase reading)
V - integrated tradition-bearer
Self cluster - whole ordered around a mature center
Temperance (XIV) — the Golden Dawn's Sagittarius, the alchemical arrow aimed at wholeness.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.

clusters.theme: The Teacher - Transmission, Doctrine, Inherited Wisdom
clusters.description: The lineage-bearer. Hands forward ritual, doctrine, method. Wisdom as tradition rather than as discovery.
clusters.editorialNote: Retained as distinct from Sage-Magician because transmission is a specific function. Overlap named; merge declined with reason.

archetypes.note: Transmits the method, gives the talisman
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Vogler (2007); Campbell (1949) on the Wise Old Man

archetypes.note: V - the true teacher, the inherited rite
Self cluster - wisdom shaped into doctrine
Type 1 - moral authority; dual-home with Threshold-Guardian
Fe/Ni - natural pedagogue
Te/Si - institutional teacher
The Hierophant's seat (V) — the keeper of what is handed down, tradition made flesh.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.

archetypes.note: The Hermit's discipline turned outward — knowledge refined into method others can follow.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: Precession has pulled the tropical signs ~24° off the constellations, so sidereal (Vedic) practice already disputes what sign this is.

clusters.theme: The Boy-Hero - Proving, Striving, Pre-Initiation Ambition
clusters.description: The un-initiated striver. Ambition without yet the ballast of maturity. The one cluster that honors developmental-stage as a structural axis.
clusters.editorialNote: Jungian Hero and Achiever promoted to primary (both moved from Warrior). ENTJ removed (triple-assignment).

archetypes.note: Boy in fullness (Warrior family) - striving before initiation
Ego cluster - the young proving self before the return
Type 3 - shame-driven image-performance; proves worth through doing
Boy in fullness (Magician family) - clever, untempered knower
Before the ordeal - untested form
VII - young will, untested drive
Se/Ti - ambitious young operator
The hero at the starting line — untested nerve, the departure before the wound.
archetypes.dissent: The Jungian reading of the signs is a 20th-century retrofit — Rudhyar (1936) and Greene grafted Jung onto a divinatory frame; the resemblance is borrowed vocabulary, not independent convergence.

clusters.theme: The Liminal Passage - The Between-Place Where Form Dissolves
clusters.description: A structural region every system names but none owns. Between departure and return; where the ego cannot recognize itself; where form dissolves and reforms.
clusters.editorialNote: NEW. Mission 8 feature-space finding + Mission 9 meta-pattern. A fourth pole alongside departure/ordeal/return. Members also dual-home in parent clusters.
clusters.adversarialNote: Offered as hypothesis. If it doesn't hold up in use, it collapses back to Shapeshifter/Death-Rebirth.

archetypes.note: XVIII - the dream-country; form dissolving
XII - willing suspension; the inverted viewpoint
X - fortune's turn
XIII - the crossing itself
XV - bound in the passage
XVI - the collapse that IS the liminal moment
The uncertain ally whose shape won't settle
Boundary-crossing function
At the initiation passage
Type 4's between-world feeling (phenomenology, not arrow-location)
archetypes.dissent: Arrow-based membership rejected (Naranjo disavowal).

archetypes.note: The Moon (XVIII) — the Golden Dawn's Pisces, the mystic's water where the path is felt, never seen.
archetypes.primarySourceCitation: Golden Dawn, Book T (c. 1888); Waite, Pictorial Key to the Tarot (1910)
archetypes.dissent: Precession has pulled the tropical signs ~24° off the constellations, so sidereal (Vedic) practice already disputes what sign this is.

developmentalStage.label: Developmental stage
developmentalStage.note: Continuous pre-initiation → striving → liminal → integrating → integrated. Masculine-coded in its dominant formulation; see /about/counter-canon.

endStates.tarot: the World reopening to the Fool

affectCenter.label: Affect center (Enneagram, extended)
affectCenter.note: Enneagram's three-center model leaves desire homeless without a fourth tint. Added per meta-pattern #8.

relationalStance.label: Horney's three moves
relationalStance.note: Karen Horney via Riso-Hudson; the single most portable structural unit in the corpus.

openQuestions: Does Liminal Territory hold up as a fourth structural pole, or collapse back to parent clusters?
Is the counter-canon (Bolen/Murdock/Estés) at true parity, or risks repeating the appendix-pattern Murdock named?
If the developmental arc is masculine-coded, is explicit labeling sufficient - or does presenting it at all harm non-male readers?
Should MBTI be demoted given its psychometric status? We retain it for pedagogical accessibility, with caveats.
Should Hero's-Journey cast be framed as Vogler's flexible functions throughout, or Campbell's character-types where tradition does?
Who owns Capricorn — Greene's Senex-Sovereign or the Golden Dawn's Devil? The map keeps it in both.
If the zodiac only speaks 'archetype' because Rudhyar imported Jung in 1936, is its convergence with the other systems evidence of anything but borrowing?

systemBlindSpots.kwml: Authored for mature men and explicitly restricted to that audience. Bipolar shadow theory contested in depth-psychology literature. Feminine quaternio had to be supplied by Stone, Bolen, and later authors.
systemBlindSpots.jungian: Primarily Pearson-Mark on this site - commercially adapted from Pearson's 1986 developmental Hero Within and 1991 twelve-archetype expansion into the 2001 brand wheel. The Drum (2025) calls the wheel 'pseudoscience' when marketed as Jungian. Brewster's Racial Complex (2020) names the focused-avoidance of race.
systemBlindSpots.enneagram: Disputed provenance (Ichazo, Naranjo, Sufi/Gurdjieff). RHETI underperforms against Big Five. 87% pre-test self-prediction creates Barnum risk. Integration/disintegration arrows were later disavowed by Naranjo.
systemBlindSpots.mbti: Most psychometrically contested system here. Type-stability across retests weaker than the system implies; corporate use widely criticized. Retained for pedagogical accessibility as a doorway to Jung's four functions.
systemBlindSpots.heros-journey: Sustained folklorist critique (Dundes, Toelken, Ellwood) of universality-by-cherry-picking. Campbell's Jungian credentials contested (Segal, Rensma, IAJS). Vogler's flexible-function framing preferred over character-type framing. Murdock's Heroine's Journey (1990) at /about/counter-canon as first-class parallel.
systemBlindSpots.tarot: Jung wrote essentially nothing on Tarot - one 1933 aside and a single experiment he reported as failed. 'Jungian Tarot' is a 1970–87 American retrofit (Gray, Nichols, Pollack, Arrien). Cardinality mismatches preclude principled 1-to-1 mapping. Pollack later complicated her own Jungian framing as a trans author.
systemBlindSpots.astrology: Astrology is the only system here that makes an empirical claim about the sky, and that claim has failed every controlled test — Carlson's Nature double-blind (1985), Dean & Kelly's time-twins (2003), the collapsed Mars effect. Its Jungian vocabulary is a 20th-century retrofit (Rudhyar 1936, Greene 1976) grafted onto a divinatory tradition Jung himself read as projected psychology, never celestial fact; even Tarnas, its most rigorous modern defender, found no signal in the signs and staked everything on the planets. And the zodiac's own house is divided: precession has pulled the tropical signs ~24° off the constellations, so sidereal practitioners already disagree about what sign you are.
